answer:
In "The Seafarer," the exiled speaker projects onto the lost homeland (the comforts of land-based life) images of communal warmth, sensory pleasures, and social bonds that starkly contrast his harsh sea existence. He evokes "luxury" on land unknown to the sea-weary wanderer, where one might enjoy the "laughter of men," "sipping of mead," harp music, "receiving rings," "joy in a wife," and "delight in the world."[^1] The homeland appears as secure "strongholds," blossoming "groves," fair "boroughs," and bright "meadows"—a fertile, ordered world that "rushes onward" and tempts the soul even as exile's "paths" demand endurance.[^2] This projection underscores personal loss: kinsmen, hall-life, and earthly joys are absent at sea, replaced by bird cries mistaken for human merriment and relentless natural assault.[^3] Yet the poem pivots to spiritual transcendence, deeming heavenly "joys of the Lord" superior to this transient land.[^4]

Iqbal's "Saqaliya," by contrast, stages exile from a collective, historical homeland—Sicily as the "grave of Hejazi civilization" and "cradle" of a once-mighty Islamic culture. The speaker (personifying his tearful eye) projects onto this lost place a heroic, world-shaking past: "desert-dwellers" whose ships treated the sea as a "playground," swords nesting "lightning," and presence that sent "tremors through the courts of emperors."[^5] It was a site of renewal, freeing humanity from "chains of superstition," resounding with the "thunderous" Takbir call, and radiating "world-consuming beauty... a fire to behold."[^6] Sicily symbolizes vanished imperial splendor, now mourned amid ruins whose "silence... holds its own eloquence," with the poet inheriting this sorrow to carry as a "gift" to Hindustan.[^7]

**Key contrasts in projection:** The Seafarer's lost place is intimate and domestic—hall joys and kin amid nature's cycle—fueling individual resignation to divine fate.[^8] Iqbal's is epic and civilizational—Muslim conquest's vibrancy reduced to decay—evoking communal revival and the poet's duty to lament for a distant audience. Both use maritime imagery (sea toil vs. sea-conquering ships) to frame exile's pain, mourning earthly transience: the Seafarer's "pride of earth's kingdoms" fallen, akin to Iqbal's silenced Takbir over Sicilian shores.[^9] Where the Seafarer seeks eternal heaven, Iqbal implies renewal through memory and verse.

[^1]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-889339](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-889339).
[^2]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-f52e2e](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-f52e2e); [p-bfb487](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-bfb487).
[^3]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-8c53e7](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-8c53e7); [p-549618](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-549618).
[^4]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-eff626](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-eff626).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [p-08760e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e); [p-13e58a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-13e58a); [p-ac137c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-ac137c).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [p-82cbe3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-82cbe3).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [p-3ff1f9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-3ff1f9); [p-61a01e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-61a01e).
[^8]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-4d6f48](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-4d6f48).
[^9]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, [p-4ea5bb](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-4ea5bb); Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [p-26728b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-26728b).
